evidence.title: Low back pain: Learn More – Medication for the treatment of chronic back pain - InformedHealth.org - NCBI Bookshelf
evidence.snippet: Only use them in addition to other treatments such as exercise therapy. Only use them for a short while (not over the long term) – for example, when the pain is particularly bad. Take the lowest possible dose. Be aware of possible interactions with other medications. You can ask about this at a pharmacy, for example. It is also a good idea to ask your doctor whether there are any health reasons why you shouldn't take the medication. This particularly applies to older people and those who have other medical problems too.

evidence.title: ACP Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Low Back Pain | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Recommendation 6: In combination with information and self-care, the use of medications with proven benefits should be considered. Before beginning treatment, physicians should evaluate the severity of the patient's baseline pain and functional deficits and the potential benefits and risks of treatment, including the relative lack of long-term effectiveness and safety data. In most cases, acetaminophen or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the first-line medication options. (Strong recommendation, moderate-quality evidence)

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old male presenting with low back pain that started about a week ago. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the spine with radiation down the right side into the right buttock, but not beyond. There is no numbness, tingling, or weakness in the legs or feet. He denies bladder or bowel dysfunction, although he had mild constipation last week which he does not believe is related. The pain began after lifting a microwave while helping his daughter move. He has no history of trauma, prior back problems, surgeries, or chronic conditions such as arthritis or osteoporosis. He has tried icing and acetaminophen with minimal relief. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, or weight loss. He reports no difficulty walking or balance issues.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute mechanical low back pain with radicular symptoms limited to the right buttock, likely due to a lumbar strain or possible mild nerve root irritation without neurological deficit.
Differential:
1. Lumbar muscle strain or ligamentous injury
2. Lumbar radiculopathy (sciatic nerve irritation without neurological deficit)
3. Sacroiliac joint dysfunction
4. Early lumbar disc herniation without nerve root compression

P: 
- First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6 to 8 hours as needed for pain for up to 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of gastrointestinal bleeding or renal impairment; patient has no known renal or liver disease; allergy to penicillin does not affect NSAID use.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if new onset weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or worsening pain occurs; otherwise, follow up in 1-2 weeks if no improvement.
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flag symptoms; imaging reserved for persistent or worsening symptoms or neurological deficits.
